keyTakeaway: Formula: **°F = °C × 9/5 + 32; K = °C + 273.15**. Just input your temperature value and instantly convert between all three scales.

Understanding temperature scales in different contexts
explanation:
**Unit conversions** form the foundation of science, engineering, and daily life. The metric system (SI) is the international standard, while the US and some other nations maintain imperial units for historical and practical reasons.

## How It Works

Enter your temperature value and select which scale you're converting from and to. The calculator applies standard conversion formulas and alerts you to any values outside typical ranges.

## Important Notes

This tool is for reference purposes. For critical applications (medical, legal, or professional), consult with a qualified specialist. Data is current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: Absolute zero is 0 K, equivalent to -273.15°C or -459.67°F. It's the lowest possible temperature.

faq.q: What are the freezing and boiling points of water?
faq.a: Water freezes at 0°C (32°F / 273.15 K) and boils at 100°C (212°F / 373.15 K).

faq.q: How do I convert Fahrenheit to Celsius?
faq.a: Subtract 32 from the Fahrenheit value, then multiply by 5/9: °C = (°F - 32) × 5/9

faq.q: How do I convert Celsius to Fahrenheit?
faq.a: Multiply the Celsius value by 9/5, then add 32: °F = °C × 9/5 + 32

faq.q: What is Kelvin and why is it used in science?
faq.a: Kelvin is the SI unit for temperature. It's used in science because it's an absolute scale—starting at absolute zero—making calculations more straightforward.
Normal body temperature is around 37°C (98.6°F). A fever is typically considered 38°C (100.4°F) or higher.
Yes, this calculator is completely free with no signup or registration required.

faq.q: Is my data stored or shared?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen in your browser. We don't send or store any of your personal data.
